_meta.purpose: Reference data for all playable/AI car classes in F1 25 (base game + 2026 Season Pack DLC). Consumed by the sim engine for pace/tyre-wear baselines, not by any UI or math logic directly.

confidenceLegend.confirmed: Directly stated by an official EA/F1/FIA source or well-documented real-world regulation.
confidenceLegend.reasonable_estimate: Inferred from confirmed facts plus general motorsport knowledge; not stated verbatim by a primary source.
confidenceLegend.placeholder: No usable source found yet; needs follow-up research or a sim-side default.

corrections.2026-07-12_apxgp_folded_into_f1_2025: APXGP was previously modeled as its own top-level car class (id 'apxgp', sibling to f1_2025/f1_2026/f2/f1_world_car). A user correctly flagged this as wrong: 'that's just an F1 2025 car.' Verified via research (see the apxgp team entry's notes under classes[0].teams) -- in the Career Mode/My Team gameplay this app models, APXGP uses F1 25's standard My Team/multiplayer car model with an APXGP livery, not a distinct chassis; only the six scripted movie-tie-in missions get a bespoke car model, and those are out of scope for this app's season/track/tier strategy flow. Folded apxgp into f1_2025.teams (same treatment as Konnersport, which was already correctly modeled this way) and removed it as a standalone class. This is a breaking change for sim's CarClassKey union and CAR_CLASSES record (src/sim/constants.ts), and for visual's DATA_ID_TO_CAR_CLASS_KEY/CAR_CLASS_TO_DATA_ID mappings and class-select screen (src/lib/dataAdapters.ts, src/lib/raceSimAdapter.ts, src/screens/CarClassTrackSelectScreen.tsx) -- coordinated directly with both teammates rather than editing their files.

classes.name: F1 2025 Season (base game)
classes.description: The 10 real-world 2025 F1 constructors, base game content. This is the pace/tyre baseline every other class' strategy assumptions get compared against.

teams.officialName: Aston Martin Aramco Formula One Team
Visa Cash App RB Formula One Team
teams.notes: Sponsor-naming churns mid-season historically; re-verify before each season update.
Transitions to Audi works team for 2026 — see f1_2026 class.

Fictional 11th team from Braking Point, selectable as an alternative 11th-team slot to APXGP in Driver Career/My Team. Principal Casper Akkerman, liaison Devon Butler; drivers Callie Mayer and Aiden Jackson in the Braking Point narrative. Strategy-relevant baseline: game narrative frames Konnersport as a genuine title threat to Red Bull, i.e. a Top Tier-capable car once its own career arc develops, not a fixed backmarker.

Fictional team from the 2025 Apple Original Films movie 'F1', selectable as an alternative 11th-team slot to Konnersport in Driver Career/My Team (Iconic Edition / Iconic Edition Upgrade content). Drivers: Sonny Hayes (Brad Pitt) and Joshua Pearce (Damson Idris). CORRECTED 2026-07-12 (was previously modeled as its own top-level car class, id 'apxgp' — see _meta.corrections below for why that was wrong): in the ongoing Career Mode/My Team gameplay this app models, the APXGP car uses F1 25's standard My Team/multiplayer car model with an APXGP livery/sponsor skin — mechanically the same chassis as any other f1_2025 team, not a distinct performance class. (APXGP does get a bespoke, uniquely-modeled car in the six movie-inspired scenario missions — Silverstone intro free to all, then Monza/Zandvoort/Mexico/Spa/Abu Dhabi as Iconic Edition content — but those are one-off scripted missions outside the season/track/tier strategy flow this app is built around, so that distinction isn't strategy-relevant here.) No official pace baseline published for the Career Mode version either way; movie narrative frames APXGP as a struggling backmarker team on an underdog redemption arc, so Backmarker-to-Midfield is a reasonable tier-slider default pending an explicit dev statement, same treatment as Konnersport's narrative-driven default — reasonable_estimate, not confirmed.

classes.strategyNotes: Pace order is not fixed in-game (depends on user's own team/upgrades in career modes); for AI-recruited or non-player teams the game's own AI pace model governs. Treat per-team absolute pace as NOT something this app should hardcode — use the Performance Tier slider (see performance-tiers.md) instead, seeded by real-world 2025 competitive order only as flavor/default suggestion.

classes.name: F1® 25: 2026 Season Pack
classes.description: New-regulation 2026-spec cars, sold as paid DLC on top of F1 25. 11 real teams (Cadillac joins, Kick Sauber becomes Audi), plus My Team as a 12th slot. Same F1 25 game infrastructure, new cars/tracks/rules layered in.

teams.notes: Team identity confirmed present; exact 2026 title-sponsor string not individually re-verified per team.
Isack Hadjar confirmed partnering Verstappen for 2026.

teams.officialName: Aston Martin Aramco Formula One Team

teams.notes: Arvid Lindblad joins Liam Lawson for 2026.
Successor to Kick Sauber; Gabriel Bortoleto and Nico Hulkenberg confirmed drivers.

Brand-new 11th constructor, first major American manufacturer entry. Car: MAC-26 (Mario Andretti Cadillac). Ferrari customer power unit through 2028, own GM power unit from 2029. Drivers Sergio Perez (#11) and Valtteri Bottas (#77). As a first-year team with a customer engine and no F1-spec development history, its realistic strategy-relevant baseline is Backmarker-to-low-Midfield, not assumed competitive — flag for sim as a case where 'real-world debut team' should not default to Midfield.

regulationChanges.change: DRS removed, replaced by Overtake Mode
regulationChanges.detail: Manual overtake-assist DRS is gone. Overtake Mode gives up to 350kW/470hp of extra electrical deployment (vs. old ~120kW/161hp Overtake in 2025-spec ERS) when within 1s of the car ahead at a Detection Line, usable until the next Activation Line, battery permitting.
regulationChanges.strategyImplication: Overtake availability is now battery-state-gated rather than a fixed lap-by-lap DRS zone allowance. This makes ERS/battery management a genuine strategic resource comparable to fuel or tyre life — a driver who burns battery defending can't threaten Overtake Mode on the next lap. The sim's ERS deployment model for 2026-spec cars should treat battery state-of-charge as a stateful variable across a stint, not a fixed per-lap boost.

regulationChanges.change: Active Aerodynamics (Cornering Mode / Straight Line Mode)
regulationChanges.detail: Wings automatically shift between a high-downforce 'Cornering Mode' (engaged under braking/cornering/lift-off, and by default outside marked zones) and a low-drag 'Straight Line Mode' active only inside track-marked Straight Line Zones (functionally similar to where DRS zones used to be, but automatic, not driver-toggled the same way).
regulationChanges.strategyImplication: Because SLM is automatic and zone-locked rather than a discretionary driver choice, it doesn't add a new strategic decision point the way old DRS enabling did — but it does change straight-line top speed and drag profile per track, which affects pit-lane-loss-relative overtaking difficulty and slipstream draft value. Net effect on strategy math is likely closer to 'zone geometry changes per track' than 'a new lever the strategist controls.'

regulationChanges.change: MGU-H removed, MGU-K deployment nearly tripled
regulationChanges.detail: Real-world 2026 regs: ERS is simplified (no MGU-H) but MGU-K deployment jumps from ~120kW to 350kW, roughly 50/50 split between combustion and electrical power.
regulationChanges.strategyImplication: Higher reliance on battery deployment for pure pace (not just Overtake Mode) means energy management across a lap/stint becomes more central to laptime than in 2025-spec cars — a car that mismanages battery state loses more raw pace than a 2025 car would for the same mistake.

regulationChanges.change: Narrower 18-inch tyres, less downforce, less drag, lighter/smaller cars
regulationChanges.detail: ~30% less downforce, ~55% less drag, cars 20cm shorter, 10cm narrower, weight down ~30kg to 768kg minimum.
regulationChanges.strategyImplication: Less downforce generally means less tyre-destroying lateral load in high-speed corners but the FIA's target was closer/more raceable cars, not necessarily lower degradation — sim should not assume 2026 tyre wear is simply '2025 minus a flat percentage' without dedicated modeling once real in-game telemetry is available. Marking this as an open question for the sim teammate rather than asserting a specific wear delta.

regulationChanges.detail: Real-world 2026 regs target 50% sustainable fuel; new fuel chemistry is a known early-season performance variance risk in the real sport.
regulationChanges.strategyImplication: No confirmed in-game fuel-effect modeling difference found yet for F1 25's 2026 pack specifically (game likely abstracts this into the existing fuel-load laptime model rather than simulating fuel chemistry). Treat as flavor/lore context for the AI explainer, not a distinct sim variable, unless the sim teammate finds otherwise.

openQuestions: In-game (F1 25 2026 Season Pack specifically) laptime delta between 2025-spec and 2026-spec cars is still not directly confirmed by EA. However, real-world 2026-vs-2025 data now exists and is a reasonable placeholder basis: FIA pre-season simulation predicted 2026 cars ~1.0-2.5s slower than 2025 cars per lap (source: Nikolas Tombazis, FIA single-seater technical director, via Autosport forums thread citing FIA statements). Actual 2026 Australian GP (Melbourne) results came in slower than that prediction: George Russell's 2026 pole (1:18.518) was ~3.4s slower than Lando Norris's 2025 Melbourne pole, and Verstappen's 2026 fastest lap was ~2.1s slower than the 2025 fastest lap. Recommend sim use a reasonable_estimate range of roughly 2-3.5s slower per lap at a 'typical' circuit for 2026-spec vs 2025-spec cars if a laptime baseline is needed now, sourced to real-world 2026 season data rather than in-game telemetry, and revisit once real F1 25 2026-pack lap times are directly observable/comparable in-game.

classes.name: Formula 2 (FIA F2 Championship)
classes.description: F1 25 includes F2 as a class; the real-world championship has run a single chassis, the Dallara F2 2024, across 2024, 2025 and 2026 with no replacement announced — so 'F2 2024 vs F2 2026 chassis' is very likely NOT a real distinction the game makes (there is only one chassis in this window). Flagging this as a likely correction to the brief's assumption rather than confirming two separate in-game F2 classes.

chassis.name: Dallara F2 2024
chassis.detail: 3.4L turbocharged Mecachrome engine, no power steering, in use for the 2024, 2025, and 2026 F2 seasons. Designed to visually/structurally resemble contemporary F1 cars.
chassis.strategyImplication: Single-spec chassis means F2's tyre/pace baseline is far more homogeneous across the grid than F1 — driver/team skill dominates over car variance. The performance-tier slider matters less for F2 strategically than for the F1 classes; still useful for AI opponent difficulty scaling, but shouldn't be read as 'car development tier' the way it is for F1.

classes.openQuestion: Whether F1 25 / the 2026 Season Pack actually labels F2 content as a distinct '2024' vs '2026' chassis in its UI is unconfirmed — no source found describing two separate F2 car classes in-game. Sim/AI should NOT assume two distinct F2 tyre/pace profiles exist until this is confirmed from in-game UI or patch notes; treat F1 25's F2 as one class pending correction.

classes.name: Driver Icons / Legends
classes.description: Legendary real-world drivers recruitable into current-spec cars in Driver Career and My Team (both by the player and, new in F1 25, by AI-controlled teams).
classes.strategyNotes: Icons drive whatever current-spec car they're recruited into (their own team's real 2025/2026-spec car) — there is no separate 'Icon car class' with its own pace/tyre model. Callum Voisin and Matias Zagazeta are original fictional legends created for the game (F1 25 debut), not real historical drivers; the other eight are real. This class should be modeled purely as a driver-skill layer on top of whichever F1 2025/2026 team car they're placed in, not a distinct car-class entry in sim's pace tables. AUDITED 2026-07-12 (coordinator's cross-class consistency check, prompted by the APXGP correction below): re-confirmed this is still correctly modeled — 'icons' was already excluded from sim's CarClassKey union and filtered out of visual's class-select list precisely because it isn't a distinct performance class. No change needed here; noted for the record that this entry was re-checked, not just left stale.

classes.name: F1 World Car
classes.description: A generic, non-team-specific car used in F1 World mode (the game's central hub for solo/multiplayer/Ranked activity), separate from any real 2025/2026 team livery. Players customize and upgrade this car within F1 World progression; Ranked and some F1 World events lock it to fixed setups for competitive parity.
classes.strategyNotes: No official baseline pace tier found published by EA. Because F1 World explicitly exists to create a setup-neutral, fair-matchmaking competitive environment (fixed setups in Ranked, License-based skill matchmaking), its car is best modeled as a fixed Midfield baseline by default — not because that's confirmed, but because a competitively-neutral generic car with progression-based upgrades logically starts below Top Tier and is designed not to feel like a Backmarker either. Sim's placeholder of Midfield for F1 World is a reasonable default; this is not yet a confirmed fact and should be revisited if EA publishes concrete stats.
classes.tierSliderAppliesResearch2026_07_12: A user flagged that F1 World cars should have no performance-tier variance at all (all identical pace), which would mean removing the tier slider for this class entirely. Investigated and did NOT confirm this -- found evidence of real progression-driven pace variance in standard F1 World play: F1 World has a License/'Technical Level' progression stat and equippable Vehicle Parts that materially affect car performance (EA's own car-setups help article documents Vehicle Parts affecting handling/tyre-wear stats; multiple EA Forums bug reports -- e.g. 'F1 25 World Car Slow At 600-1000 Tech', 'F1 25 - F1 World Car Extremely Slow Despite High Technical Level' -- corroborate that Technical Level is meant to scale car pace, i.e. genuine upgrade-driven variance exists, not a fixed baseline). The ONLY place cars are confirmed locked to a fixed/identical setup is Ranked mode and 'some F1 World events' specifically, for competitive parity -- not F1 World as a whole. This is structurally the same shape as F1 2025/2026's My Team pace drifting across a season, which the plan already models via the tier slider -- so keeping tierSliderApplies=true for f1_world_car is the factually correct call, not an oversight to fix. Recommend the tier slider here be read as 'where is my F1 World Technical Level/car progression currently,' same interpretive frame as My Team. If a future 'Ranked mode' toggle is ever built, that specific mode (not the class) is where fixed-parity behavior belongs -- flagged as a possible v2 UX idea, not implemented now since it's out of scope for a single boolean fix.
